rationale: The citing paper uses Zauberman et al. (2009) for more than background mention: it explicitly adopts their discount-rate elicitation approach in multiple lab studies (including the discount-rate calculation) to construct control variables. It also cites that work when discussing hyperbolic/time-discounting as an alternative account for observed choices. These controls are central to the paper's claim that period-level bracketing effects are not driven by discounting differences.
relation_description: The citing paper applies Zauberman et al.'s time-discounting measurement approach to operationalize participants' discount rates and test a competing explanation. It then uses those tests to argue that its hassle-timing effects persist beyond standard discounting-based accounts.
